message:
fix(mux-server/ossl): add wall-clock watchdog around TLS handshake to defeat slow-loris drip

Per-syscall `set_read_timeout` / `set_write_timeout` on the TCP socket
only caps the time each individual read/write blocks. A slow-loris
attacker can keep the handshake alive indefinitely by dripping one byte
every (TLS_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT - ε) seconds: each syscall returns with
progress before the deadline, but the overall handshake never
completes. Result: the mux thread holding `acceptor.accept` stays
pinned forever, consuming one slot of the server's connection budget
per attacker.

Fix: spawn a watchdog thread that holds a cloned TCP handle and a
shared completion flag. The watchdog parks in a 250ms-poll loop until
either (a) the flag flips — handshake completed or errored, exit
clean — or (b) the wall-clock deadline `now + TLS_HANDSHAKE_TIMEOUT`
elapses, in which case it calls `shutdown(Shutdown::Both)` on the
cloned handle. That forces the blocked read/write inside
`acceptor.accept` to return an error, which aborts the handshake and
releases the mux thread.

Key correctness points:
  - `try_clone()` is called before `acceptor.accept` consumes `stream`,
    so the watchdog has a live handle.
  - The completion flag is stored with Release on the accept path and
    loaded with Acquire in the watchdog, so the ordering is well-defined.
  - `drop(watchdog)` is non-blocking (JoinHandle drop is detach in std),
    so a fast handshake returns immediately; the last 250ms tick of the
    watchdog runs to completion silently.
  - Fast-path cost is one thread spawn + one cloned fd per connection,
    matching the order of magnitude of the existing TLS accept itself.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
